speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Ruth Leland took place at her home in Juliaetta on May 25th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That was in 1906.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a big part of the town?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Yes, it was considered the thing in town. You see, there were a lot of people here retaking the treatments, as well as several students he had that he was teaching the business. And it was quite a big thing. And I don't know if you've probably been told where it was. The building still stands. Yes, somebody lives in it now. It's a home.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, you go up this block and the next block and it's up around the corner of that creek. And you just put a building up there. And, but, checking back on things. I remember the events that touched me close to eight. There was a family living in there in 1909. So sometime in that time he must have left, I imagine about 1908. But I wasn't living in town then, see. In fact, this is why I can't help you too much. I have never really been a part of Juliaetta. That isn't what you can count the past few years when I've lived here, stay with me. I was here about six months that winter, absorbed in school. And then we moved to this place between here and Kendra, two miles out of town.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I'm giving this to you all background so you'll understand this is not for public education.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, let me ask you about what you do remember about the Foster School. Because there are many people that were around the country at all prior to 1910. I think that's the reason why it's both well known and yet we know very little about it. Oh yes, I don't mean that.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I don't mean that it brought any great population into the town or anything of the kind. But it was considered the big thing of the town, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it helped that it then was kind of building up the town when it was here?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: No, I don't. I really don't.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It brought in outsiders.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who mostly stayed for a cure and then left?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Yes, that was all.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It didn't bring in any people who left here. And when he left, he took with him these students who had left here that were, I think about oh possibly three or four.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of cure was it? Was there more than one?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: He claimed to have a cure for cancer.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That is skin cancer. And some kind of a saab or paste or something of that kind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A what? Salve?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Some kind of a salve or paste or something of that kind that he put on.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And he claimed cures for it, which is quite possible because there are many types of skin cancers and different things that work on them. But as far as I know, he did not claim to have it. Now this is as far as I know, he did not claim to have any cure for the interior cancers.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I see.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: He was not really, as I understand it, an MD. He was more of an osteopath.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I think he claimed, I'm just, this is off the top of my head because I really don't know too much about it. But I think he claimed to have run into this cure accidentally. That's right.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: He was very much, very strong on nutrition and that type of thing. And natural foods and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Oh yes, he did a lot of good with that. I'm rather in favor of that kind of thing myself.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well I had read in that old edition of the Juliet of the Sun, you know that one from 19, or you know right around 1910 that came out when they mentioned the school in there. I think they said that he gave lectures regularly.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That kind of surprised me too to read that. I was wondering if they were really for the town or just for his patients or if people would just come and listen to him.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I think it was in the sanitary inn, but anyone was welcome to come, who cared to come.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: The sanitary inn, that's what they called it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it had a resident population?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I mean people living right there.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It's a big house.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And it was always known as a sanitary inn. And then there were a lot of the people. And that way, I suppose it did help the town. There were a lot of people, and he's staying in homes too. Getting room and board in homes. And then going over for treatment. And then going over for their treatment and the lectures and so forth.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: In that Juliaetta Sun, they mentioned a number of other forms of treatment besides the cancer one. They mentioned, I don't remember exactly what all they said, but there was, I think, use of magnetism. Oh yes. And self -healing, I think, too, was a part of this idea.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I told you he was nasty at that. I know he...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Did you know of anybody that would say, or that said that they were cured by...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Oh, there were quite a lot of the problems he did. And as I say, I think he probably did cure some of them, even if they had cancer. Because I think, I know there's so many different types of them. Since I have had a variety of course, surgery for cancer, I'm quite interested in that type of thing.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I imagine, and Phil, I know some of them used to call him the rubbing doctor. So I know he must have given that type of treatments.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Like most of the nutritionists, they believe quite strongly in the self -help. Almost all of them do. That is the idea is, if you think this is going to help, it will. If you don't, it won't help, no matter how good it is. And it is perfectly true. Most medical doctors, practically all medical doctors, do the same. Believe in the same. They know that a person's attitude towards whatever treatment they're getting is going to have an awful lot to do with how much good they get off of the treatment.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: You know, I had the idea that this interest in natural foods was more kind of a recent interest. I mean, it used to be everybody used natural foods, I thought, but didn't talk about it. Nowadays, they talk about it a lot and don't use them as much as they used to, with all the cellophane, wrap, prepackaged foods instead. You know what I mean. But it's quite interesting to think that natural foods would have been a part of his method for staying well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear of the Seventh -day Adventist Church?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Yes.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They have taught natural foods from the very start of the Church.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That's quite true. It's not too new, I think. That's quite true. It has not got popular like it is recently. The last few years it's got very popular.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the Seventh -day Church at all known around here in those days?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I know Troy, it's had a fairly strong group at Troy and at Viola, both. And it had that academy in Viola in the early days. But I just was thinking, I hadn't really heard about it from in this area as being...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, there hasn't been a Seventh -day Church here in the last few years. There's one in a small church here now, composed mainly of people who have come in from outside.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That is one thing I can tell you about the Church.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, the early churches we're very interested in. We find all too little about the early religion in the country.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, I don't know who was the first one here. I very much suspect the presbyterians since they started the thing. Down in around Swarthmore, Midgapoy, and all.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: When I came here, there had already been a first -degree church, but I don't know how long. They had a building, which would be terracore across from the school up here. It burnt down, I think, greater. And just before, it is not long before I came here, they built another church down here, which eventually was made into the apartments where Staggs lives, and sold that to the Lutherans. To the Lutheran church. And there was also a Catholic church here in town, but I don't know how far back that needed to be. It was up here, I don't know what happened to it, I guess maybe it burned down or something. I don't know, I really don't know.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But, the presbyterians shared a ministry with Kendrick. And Kendrick wanted to offer morning services, so he would come down for Sunday night services. And walk down from Kendrick's, which I don't know where he didn't offer. He didn't have transportation otherwise. But he would walk down and walk back. I got to know him quite well because he was English, my mother was English, and he used to stop halfway down for a cup of tea with Mother. We got very friendly, he was a very nice man. Not young, anything. But then in the morning services, they had a union, a Sunday school, of practically all the turkeys in town. And there were several ministers, a Methodist minister,

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: and another branch of Lutheran minister, an American Lutheran minister.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And you be ministers,

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: and I forget, there were others would come in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these ministers shared with Juliaetta as well? Shared by both towns?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Some of them, yes.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Yes,

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: they were regular ministers. I mean, they came on a regular basis.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: When you say regular basis, that's a...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: The same one, the same one came.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how, it wouldn't be every week, like the presbyterians?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: No, they would take them, I think there were about three of them, that came regularly on a specified sentence. But now to go back to Dr. Foster, he had been in MF2. He was a member of the Christian church. And I expect they had, this is just before my time, I expect that they had a minister of that denomination too. But he fell out with a bunch up there. This is something you may not hear from other people, but you may not know too much about it. So I may not want to talk about it, but he fell out with a church up there. I heard, because he couldn't run it, and now he's Dr. Foster, I imagine that was the reason. So with his own money, he built a church down here. I just found this Lutheran church, that there, Christian church, his nomination. The part is minister of his own. Who did get there, but came up to preach. And it was going, when I got here, I used to attend church here, part of the time. And then I attended the other one too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now this minister was visiting minister or resident?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: No, he wasn't resident. He was visiting. Now I don't know if it was possible that he might have resided up with Dr. Foster. There's faith up there, but it was my understanding, remembrance, that he was not resident, but came in from one of the other churches. And so there were two Christian Protestant churches going here. Then the Lutheran church burned down, and they built this church over here that is in the recent news. But eventually, members of the Christian church moved away, or weren't very enthusiastic or something, after Dr. Foster left. And it was hard for them to get ministers here, but they would get ministers here at the evening. And they got into the bad tests, who had a foreign out with the... And it was Presbyterian minister up there then, and decided they wanted their own church. So they bought the Christian church and came down here.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: So then after, of course, this is getting into warm autumn, the elected president had moved away. They died out. Decided there wasn't any... by the way, that is the church of a minister. And decided they couldn't go up anymore.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But they dropped their meetings.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And the Nazarenes came in and started meetings. And started a church which met in this building down here, which was a bank and various other things. And now has apartments over it, right next door to the service station.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And they cried when they outgrew that, saw their manifest, they were going to have a church for... They were getting quite a lot of people. They tried to buy a Baptist church. And the Baptist church, that is the local church, is perfectly willing to sell to them. But, because they borrowed money from the national board to buy it, they had to put the deed in the hands of the national board. And the national board would not sell it. And the Nazarenes, some of them, have always been a lost source of Baptist. Because they went for the Baptist church in Juliette, Advent. Nothing to do with that. They were quite willing to sell, and would have been glad to sell to them. And it was a nice church, very well equipped and everything.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But the Baptist maintained their congregation after...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Until they moved away or died too. I think I am possibly the only person in town who ever belonged to that Baptist church. I joined it when I was a girl. I went to the other place later. The man...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It was sold to a local man, and he wrecked it for the material in it. He left from the floor.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: So, and the Nazarenes built their church on up further up here. The Seventh -day Adventists and the others came in from outside and decided they wanted to sell a church. There were quite a lot of them then, and so they built a church just this side of it. And now, that is the history of church denominations in any town.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They start, they grow, they thrive, and they die.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And somebody else comes in. I think they simply get to thinking too much of their church as their denomination, and they can't use them anymore. That's the way I see it. And so, the Seventh -day Adventists, who were most of the people, retired, could come here anyway. There's a lot of them gone. They still hold their services every Sabbath with a minister from Christ.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But even if they're not, the Mormons are using their church.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Every so often, they have services there.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: So there's your history, your church history. That's fascinating, just fascinating. Many of that you can use of course. That's just fascinating.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be okay if I take that kettle off?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because it's the whistling.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Just one thing that I didn't get in there.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: The large pay owing, but this one minister came in, I was thinking. The Methodists drew out from the union up there too. And they built this church on Main Street.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That cement church.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That was the Seventh Methodist.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: This from the union at Kendrick.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But the union, no, it went up here. It was in the Presbyterian church.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I see, I see.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: So the church in Kendrick was the North Methodist, or it had been. And this was the Southern Methodist.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what were the services then like they are now?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Like they would be in any small church you go into. Some of them very formal, some of them rather informal. But I'm going the general line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the pioneers, the earlier settlers, do you think that they took their religion more seriously than people tend to nowadays? Or wouldn't it be much different?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: People are people.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Circumstances and surroundings don't change them a great deal.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Some of them took it very seriously. Some of them had no more use for it than a lot of people today do.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Human nature is the same as it has been from the start. You find all kinds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much of the community life was centered around the churches?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well,

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: looking back, I don't believe there was a great deal more than there is now. There was to this extent, there was nothing. One of them got in a revivalist for a meeting. Why, practically everybody's apt to go because there wasn't anything else to go to.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Whether you could call that centering or not, I don't know. It was just something to go to.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: So they went.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: One thing we used to have in Jumu 'ah was our big Sunday school meetings.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: We used to have a beautiful park down here where the mill is now.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Some of the Sunday schools got the idea of going down there on a Sunday that is called Children's Day and having a union program and a picnic. So they invited nearby Sunday schools and they came. And the next year, more of them wanted to come. They'd get an outside speaker.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And then the different Sunday schools would furnish items for the program. And we'd have an all -morning program and then a big dinner and sometimes an afternoon one.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Usually the speaker would be in the afternoon. And it grew until one year. There were well over a thousand down there from the Sunday school from way all around. And those days they mainly came in horse and buggy, although it was getting so that they were a few cars. And it was just really quite a big affair.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: You know when you say Children's...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: The third Sunday in June for many of the Protestant churches has always been a third at Children's Day. With special programs from the Sunday school and special recognition, the classes and all of the Sunday schools, the graduates from each class are moved up to the next one. And usually the whole class is just moved up. Primary will be called junior the next year. The beginners are moving the primary and so forth.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I think about what the children would do in the program.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the kind of presentations that they'd be making in those days?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They'd have...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They would have their recitations and their songs and their drills. Oh, how I had drills and drills. There's something on that or some of those things down there. A whole group of them through most intricate drills, you know. Perhaps it's rags or sometimes it's okay if ours are rears or something. Marching in and out. You don't think there's some work to get those up. And usually I had to manufacture them myself. Once I did.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Each school was supposed to furnish at least two numbers. Not more than four and finally had to amount to the two when it got so terribly big. Because they get down there with an outdoor program, you see. They build a platform for it. Move an instrument down there for it. Why, each item would be pretty elaborate. But a group of songs that are real or possibly will play with it for some kind.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It was really something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say a drill, what kind of drill would it be again?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Like any drill. Like you see a marching band stop at the speed and suddenly go through symmetric figures.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That type of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How large of an area would this draw from?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Roughly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far away would people come?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, as I say, they tend to have the transportation they have now. They would come from Troy. They would come from Southwick. Gold Hill, around up in there, Teakin. And down to an hour.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That's a pretty good, that's a pretty large area even for those days. I mean, even for nowadays. You see, it was run all day up there.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And they would bring their dinners and that. Very often, I got out there and I would put the dinners together.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And there would always be food if somebody hadn't brought something. There would always be food to spare.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: If we were going to try to know just roughly the period of time when this was going on. I know it's tough to date things like that. But, this would have been probably, well, it would have been well before World War II. Oh, yeah. This probably would have been after World War I perhaps or even before then.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, now it was between the World Wars.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I think it finished. I couldn't tell you just how many years it did last. It lasted quite a long time.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Yeah, I wonder why they stopped it. It sounds like such a great...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It stopped in the middle of World War II.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And there again, there was a misunderstanding that broke it up.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: By that time, we weren't able to use the park. For some reason, I cannot remember why we couldn't use the park then. I know the last one we had was in a big Teotokwis tent, set in the middle of the square right up here.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And, I forget who the speaker was. But, I know that those in charge said that they got order from the government if they used that speaker. They must also use a group of GIs for back on fertile.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: So, apparently the speaker's HO, which was not... That was during the war.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Possibly they thought he was too anti -war.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they said use a group of GIs, you mean to speak?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: On the program.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was the last year?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That was the last year we had it. Perhaps he was of German descent, because I know that during the war they seemed to get some pretty strong ideas. I know.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: No, I don't think that was it. I think he was rather anti -war.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, would the speakers talk about secular subjects as well as religious ones at these...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They were entirely free to talk about anything they wanted to. In fact, they were often brought in for some special thing that you might call secular.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But, speaking of the German descent, during the war,

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: we had... That was during World War I. The Methodist minister down here was a German.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Spooked.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Quite an accent.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And, a very nice guy. He was an American citizen.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But, there was quite a bit of feeling in town about him and against him.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But, on Armistice Day, that is the false armistice, the first one that came over here at the time, you know. Everybody, although it was raining and some of us had just over the blue,

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: everybody gathered down here in the square. The pride pool was my medical thing. And, to celebrate, it was just kind of twizzery, you know. I was just out over the pool myself. Didn't have any business there. But, they all meowed around. Just had a happy time. Somebody thought we ought to sing the Star Spangled Banner.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And, everybody's bravery started off. Oh, say can you see. Everybody stopped. They couldn't talk. Except the German minister. And, he knew it all the way through.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: We sang the first verse. And, then they decided maybe they'd set her on...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Oh, I know perfectly well you do too about the hymn. My Country, Josephine. And, not many of the grown people knew it. Some of them did. But, all the kids did because all during the war, it was part of the regular program of the Sunday School, at the Union Sunday School. So, they knew it all the way through all four verses. The grown ups knew the first and that was it. But, I've laughed over that so many times. The only man who knew the national anthem was the German.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I had heard that as a part of that celebration or sometime after that, not too long, or sometime around then, that some kids had jammed a flag pole into the wall of the church or something like that to the Methodist Church. They'd done something, you know, to show...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Could be. I wouldn't know.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Someone told me about that. It sounded like a part of that same kind of sentiment that you were talking about. Where they didn't like, because he was German.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I bet kids might have done it, but I bet it was some grown ups were behind it. Because it took the kids right to... He was a very likable guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was still able to take part in the Union activities too, wasn't he, during that time?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: No, I had to write, you see.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: You see, that church is good. But, he took part when the churches went together and everything. Why he took part in that.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I had heard that there was opposition and dislike of speaking the German language during that time. I had heard that. In fact, even Frank Brockey told me that, you know, he's German, that when they would talk on the phone line in German, his family, to neighbors, when other people, you know, rubbering on the line there, didn't like that. Because they thought, well, they're talking in German.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are they talking about?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: During that period of time. So, there must have been quite a bit of, you know, wondering about it.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, we didn't have too many Germans around here at the time, but the viewing was pretty high, alright. I know the Shufers felt it, awkward, and...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But, this I heard, I have heard, that maybe Mr. Brockey might have said something about it. Kindred to check on oceans if they were going up and wipe out camera.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Maybe he wouldn't say anything about it. Oh, he probably would. I didn't ask him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they get very far?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They did get very far.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Camera got on to him. I think they figured they'd get off without too much. Yeah.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Yeah, well, the Shufers have talked to me about that kind of stuff, too. It's too bad, because when you think the people are as American as anybody else, having to, you know, having to defend their patriotism, it's just too bad.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But that racist feeling is at the bottom of so many things.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I know that my boss in the store, Joe Alexander, was a Jew. And there was a lot of people, and they wouldn't go into the store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that reason? What was he like to work for?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Fine.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Just very, very considerate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that he himself felt accepted by the community, or do you think that he kind of wondered a lot?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Oh, he was accepted by the community, but only from certain people, see. Oh, he felt it. You can't help but feel it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he do a good business?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Real good.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: There are a number of cases around the county of Jews who ran early stores. You may know of some of the others. There was Durnim and Kaufman in Moscow, and there was the Rosensteins and Levi in Genesee, too. All early -day merchants.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had Alexander been here from...was he here when you first came?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Oh, yes. So he was a pioneer merchant in the... Yes. I wonder if he built his business up right here in Juliaetta. I mean, this is where he... I imagine he did.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Of course, the Alexanders were a big firm in the from pioneer days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Same people?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Same family.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Was...do you think he was as generous with...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he give credit at the store?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See, I've heard about, for instance...have you heard of Rosenstein from Genesee?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Oh, yes. I've heard a number of people who traded at that store just swearing by the kindness and the willingness to give credit that the Rosensteins had people. So I thought that's kind of interesting because, you know, because of some of the ideas about how Jewish people are maybe trying to, you know, get more money than others. I thought that was really interesting.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, no. Joe Alexander, he was careful, of course. I asked him who he gave credit to.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But I'm certain that there were cases when he gave credit when he knew he would never get anything out of it.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And any community project, that type of thing, he would count on him right to be women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have a family here?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Yes.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They used to have...well, this house is here now. It's a different house than that.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But after he passed away, his wife tried to continue business.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And that was just during World War I, towards the end of it. Well, it was right after World War I, the end of it. And that was when I lost my job because she couldn't afford to keep the whole crew on. And she tried to take my place herself. The aid of her bookkeeper, who had become her brother -in -law, and one man. Because they had to have a man in the grocery department. When they tried to go on, I think she might have made it, only the star burned down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just the store, or was it a regular Main Street fire?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It was just the store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they know how that fire started?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Never. We're sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By the way, was she Jewish too?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Oh no, she wasn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she a local girl?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Yes.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: She was in Johnson.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Had he died young, Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Alexander?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, yes, you might consider him a rather young man, three boys.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But he wasn't old by any means. I couldn't give you his exact age, but he was mid -aged, that's it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much Indian trade in the store? Were there many Indians?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: He had an awful lot of Indian trade.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They got to trade with him, because he'd bust with them, you know. And when the store burned down, he had over a thousand dollars, and a thousand dollars at that time was something, in Indian goods that he bought from them. See, he would buy stuff from them, beadwork and all that type of thing.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And they'd come and they'd come and quarrel over them. Just had a real good time with them. That, dealing with the Indians, the Indians at that time was basically, was something.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: You could sell an Indian, oh maybe fifty dollars worth of stuff, one item at a time. They would buy one item and pay for it. Then the next one they bought. You had to have patience up front, but it would pay off. You were willing to pull them, and they couldn't be hurried.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You couldn't get them to take the idea of just getting everything they wanted and then buying it all at once?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: No, who knows. I wonder why. I wonder why.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They wanted to know just how much each one cost, and that they weren't being cheated. I think the whites had taught them that.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They knew just what one thing was going to cost, and they could pay for it then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But if they bought several things, how would they even know that you hadn't selected an extra price on them somewhere?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They weren't that much up on our money. Oh, I can see the reason to it all right. And you couldn't sell the mammoth. You could show them stuff if they wanted it, they would buy it.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But that was it. You could tell them how good it was and that kind of thing.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But Mr. Alex Anderson used to have lots of fun with them. He'd say, this is what you need. You need this for that and the other. For all the others. He had a good time over it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that he would take more of a fuss, more attention, pay more to work with them more than the average storekeeper would?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I think so. I think that's why he had such a big trade.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: So maybe they realized or thought that he was more concerned about them. You know, thought more of them maybe.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That's what I think. I think so. Now you see, the reason I think that is a lot of those people had come clear from Godfrey to deal with him. They could have gone to the Liston just as easily. To the bigger stores down there. But there, the people wouldn't have fussed with them. In fact, they wouldn't have been welcome in the Liston.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And on about that time, there were plenty of places for the sign -up. No Indians. It wasn't too many years ago that you might find one of those signs on certain places in the list.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I know. Oh,

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: it can't be more than 15 or 20 years ago. If that long, I remember a certain restaurant in the Liston, we served only whites.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It's hard for me to, oh I can imagine it because so much I've heard about the South, but to imagine it against the Indians around here. That seems kind of very unfair. I hadn't thought of it either. It just occurred to me a couple minutes ago, sitting right next to us here.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Boy, that's really something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How were they welcome in Juliaetta? The Indians?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Yes. Oh, excepted in some places. Others didn't think they would.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They found it hard to get work here for a while.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: There's still a lot of people that say that wouldn't have an Indian work for me.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And yet, you'll hear they say the best help I ever had with Indians.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think the difference comes down to? Why some people would hold one and one the other?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Because I've heard the same kind of difference. Many people saying the Indians were really fine people, and yet a few just not having any use for them. I've always wondered why this difference. It's either one way or the other.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, it's the color of the skin.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And yet it isn't really that. It's because the color of the skin means to a lot of people a different culture from what we have. A different way of describing it. And because we think ours is the best, we don't want the other.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, they think theirs is the best, too. And we can't understand that. Because we know ours is the best.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That's what it boils down to. It's taken me 80 years and better to realize it. That's what it boils down to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the Indians that you had dealings with in the store?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I liked them very much.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I sometimes had to grab my patients with both hands. Especially if they were somewhat rushed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you consider that you knew them?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, I dealt with individuals more than families.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I had some very, very good friends among the Indians. By the way, the Indian churches would come up to our Sunday school thing, too. The Indian Sunday schools would. I remember one of the most impressive things that we ever had in one of those programs was one of the Indian men giving the Lord's Prayer in Indian Sign Language.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Believe me, when he said it, as he gave it, there wasn't a stir in that whole audience.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That was one of our big ones, too.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They would come up with their songs, sometimes in Indian, sometimes in English, and join right with the rest of the group.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Well, I want you to know that I have found, in talking to old -timers, far more liking and caring about the Indians than I ever expected when I first started talking to people. In fact, it's the rare person that doesn't have much use for them. It's the common thing that people really like them. And I like that. I'm glad to see that. That's the way people feel. I think maybe some places in the West it might not be that way, but around here. And not just around here only, but up around Derry and further north it's the same.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It has changed a great deal in the last 25 or 35 years.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Because I can remember very well when that was not the attitude at all.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And even today, there will be some movement.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Of course, people that I talk to are old -timers. I mean, they're people that have lived or knew them when they were young. And maybe they, maybe like you, other people have come to understand better through the years.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: If anyone should have had something up against the Indians and lots of people would have said, I did. For I was born in the middle of the Sioux uprising in Dakota. I was born across the island of Wyoming.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And I know that folks have told me how much trouble they had to keep baby Ruth quiet. Well, with blankets over the windows and all that. Distinguished, they wondered if one of the raiding parties would come across. They didn't. They never did.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And my folks never held it against the Indians and therefore I never did.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And when I ran Indians down here, well, they were just people.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Like other people.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It's always been my feeling about the Negroes. That these addicts, any of them, they're people.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And our guidebook tells us that God made us one God, all nations of the earth. And any doctor, any scientist will tell you it's absolutely true. You take samples of God from all of them and you can't tell the difference. No matter what color their skin is. No matter where they come from.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And that also tells us that the blood is the new guy. We know that's true. Without it, we could not have had it.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Therefore, where's the difference?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I could put on paint and change the color of my skin. I'd go out in the sun, I'd change it.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Stay indoors, I'd change it again.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: What difference does it make?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that you dealt with individuals rather than families in the store, does that mean that only the wife or the husband would come into the store to trade?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It's a general thing. They didn't bring the children in. Occasionally they did. But believe me, when they did, those children didn't touch anything.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They found it out from the parents right now.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They might have wanted something and asked about it. But no, no, no touch. They were very, very well behaved. Much better behaved than the white ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are the people that you got to know and become friends with the people that traded at the store or ones that you met through the church?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Both.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I can certainly understand why some of the resentment that recently has come out that they have every right to feel that way because of the kind of treatment that they've received. And I wonder if that could even be part of why the kids would have to behave themselves so strictly. Of course, maybe that was just the Indian way, but maybe they were... The Indian way of training them.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They don't believe in this permissiveness.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that all this country was once theirs?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It could be pretty hard, you know, to...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: There is one thing that to me shows more than anything else that they feel they are not accepting. You are very rarely... In fact, I can't say that I have ever known an Indian to speak first.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Or to all praise him.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: You speak, they're quite willing to respond. Quite willing to say yes. But you almost start.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Too much rejection.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: How would you feel under the circumstances?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's very interesting. I never thought of that and what that means.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they have retained or kept the closeness of their communities pretty much through the years?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It's hard to tell. I believe more than we realize.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And I think it is coming out right now. This new Indian movement.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: The Indians are... The different tribes are taking up the cajole, as you might say, for the other Indians. They seem to be uniting as a people far more than they have.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Do you think that their Christianity, the form that they practice, is different because of their cultural traditions than...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The usual kind? The kind that is more accepted, common around here?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm not sure I understand just what you mean.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Maybe I don't. I'm not really familiar with their...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: You mean their form of worship?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It is of all different. Just as you will find a form of worship of all different in different sections of this country, for instance, as well as in other countries. I don't suppose you have ever had the privilege, as I have had once, of going to their big tail -mikes camp meeting up here by Winchester. If you ever get a chance, go up there. They have it every year. And it is absolutely fabulous.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: For instance, in one of their prayer meetings, there will be hundreds there. They will be praying to God. Not to God, but to God. All over that group. And yet there is no sense of any confusion. I can't explain it. All these voices coming, not a unison at all, because they're all praying their own prayers. Some in English, some in Indian.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: But there's a unity about the whole thing.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Did you get it?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Explain the talk, unless it is. The OSPI himself is there. He's guiding it.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And, well, that is a thing you wouldn't ordinarily find, so you might, especially in the Pentecostal churches, find that sort of thing. But the Pentecostal churches I attended, I felt much more disunity than I did up there. They have their own way of holding their services. They're asked for hours. Most white people are very annoyed if the minister dares to speak more than 30 minutes.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Or if the service lasts over an hour, they just can't take it. Not for the Indians. They have all the time there is. They're there to worship, and they'll just keep on worshipping as long as they want to. In that way, you might say their form of purgatory is quite different to ours. But I don't think that ours is any better.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And I think that the whole thing might rest on that. The Indian feels he has all the time there is. Well, that's all any of us has. But the rest of us don't feel it. We feel we've got to get out and do things. This happened.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And the Indian thinks, what difference will it make in the long run?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He knows, perhaps better. He knows and we don't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is Presbyterianism still the strongest denomination among the Indians?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Catholicism is quite strong. Well, we've got a lot of Methodists, too.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: It would depend, I think, mainly on where you happen to be.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I was thinking of the Nez Perces, primarily. Thinking about the names of the McBeth sisters.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they here when you were first here?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Did you ever happen to...

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I never met them, but they rest all right when I can. But the Indians are quite strong on tradition. And so it would carry over into that, too.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: I'd like to ask you a little bit about the revivals. I understand, from talking to people and reading, too, about the early days, that the revivals, they seem to have been, well, more important, in that there was a lot more of them. And they were, as you mentioned before, saying that that's what there was to do. They had a great deal more participation in those days from the general population of the community. And I was wondering how frequent they were, and about them, what they were like then. Well,

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: they differed, depending on who was doing it. But as a general thing, they would... each church would have at least one a year, sometimes two. Usually they'd run on one.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And depending on the location, it would be in the summer or in the winter. Sometimes it ran on two on a summer camp meeting, when everybody went out and lived in tents and things. And then in the winter, where it would be held in the church, there would be one or more speakers each evening. Usually they would get in a main speaker. Not the pastor of the church, but a main speaker from outside. Perhaps a singer or two. Song of the Creator.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And the whole sermon would be... a whole series of sermons would be built around the thought of getting the people who were already professed Christians, but weren't working at it too hard, back where they ought to be. And getting those who had never become Christians to be saved, become Christians.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They would call it Repi -Vorce because it was the idea of Repi -Vorce. They were fighting that one to turn the street away a bit.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And they would have some service, half an hour or so, singing. And they would have the kind of work up to the meeting, and crafts and special honors. And they would have the sermon, and the speaker would make what was called the altar call, and invite all those who felt they wanted to do better, to come to the altar and pray. And other Christians would gather around with them and pray, and if they seemed to need some help and just understanding what it was all about, I'd counsel with them. They were sure that they understood it all and knew what they were doing. And, in the words that the scripture were born again, it really becomes Christians who have been saved. It really let Christ enter their hearts, take over.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: People would come from miles and miles around in the swaths in the winter. And, of course, in the summer, and as I say, cars more recently, of course. We practically every church today, every one of the Protestant churches plans on at least once during the year. But, with this change, in most of the churches today, the emphasis is really on revival, on the people who have been Christians, or claim to be Christians, and have strayed away, fallen back. And not nigger so much on the unconverted people, although they are welcomed, and if a church really gets revived, there's usually quite a lot of people converted too. Because they see there's something there that they really need, really want.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: And when you can show people you've got something they want, something that's worth having, they're going to want it and they're going to try to get it. And that is the root of revival.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Some of them were quite quiet, others got quite noisy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that answer your question?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: That goes a long way towards answering it. That's a good overview of it. A little more than I've really quite understood properly. I think this difference between today's and early day revival is quite interesting. And that's the part that I didn't really understand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When people converted then, were they often people who were nominally members of the faith, but who had not actually been born again?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Many times they were people who had never had anything to do with it whatsoever.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They'd strayed in the meeting simply because there was some place to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this mean that they wouldn't have been a member of any Christian faith at all?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: May not even have been raised in a Christian family. May have been the first time they've ever heard of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this common?

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Not at all uncommon.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: They might have been people who had gone to church or whose family had gone to church, but they've gone to Sunday school or something of the kind. And they have all backgrounds.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: Otherwise, sometimes they wouldn't have had a bit. I'm thinking now of a man who was a member of one of my churchies who told me that as a young man in Seattle, not a boy, a young man, he had strayed into, I think into a mission, some seven meeting anyway, and heard the Gospel for the first time in his life. And he was an American, born and bred. He said it was the first time they had ever heard it. He said, I knew right then it was something I wanted, something I had to have. He was converted that night. And he still lives in Lewiston now. He's still a very faithful Christian man, Christian family. Okay.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: So you see that kind of thing happens too.

speaker: Ruth Leland
words: May I ask you a very personal question?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're going to ask me what my faith is? I'm going to ask you, are you a Christian?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As a matter of fact...
